05 Deploy the Playbook - YouTube.mp4
All right, at this point, we have defined our playbooks. We've designed our playbooks. Now it is time to deploy these playbooks into the organization. Deployment is defined by three areas: training, availability, and accountability. First, let's talk about training. A finished playbook is the ideal training program for both new and existing hires. In fact, I would invest the time to train anyone who even touches a single step of a given playbook on how to execute the entire playbook. So let me give you an example. Let's say you have a playbook on how to send out your company's weekly email newsletter. And let's say one of the steps in the playbook is to secure a featured image from your graphic designer. What I want you to do is: I want you to take the time to train that graphic designer on the entire playbook.
Now I realize that he or she is only directly involved. And maybe step seven of a 20-step playbook. But not only will this cross-training create redundancies that can be super helpful if a team member is sick, quits, or... just goes on vacation, the added context will make everyone on your team more effective because everyone will get what everyone else is doing and why they're doing it. Another benefit to cross-training is the opportunity it creates for career growth on behalf of your employees. Now, in our company, we've had graphic designers ascend into marketing roles, we've had customer care reps shift into community management and social media management, and we've had copywriters pivot into commission sales roles, all because we invested the time to cross-train them. Believe me, there is literally zero downside to cross-training.
The limited time that you invest will more than pay for itself in time not lost when a critical team... member quits or has just taken some time off. More importantly, the company will see a massive and immediate bump in performance and your team will be motivated by the opportunities for career growth that cross-training enables. Again, there is zero downside and unlimited upside to cross-training, so don't skip this step. Now, let's talk about availability. Okay. So you have your company's secret sauce documented in a set of playbooks, but where do you put them? If you lock them in a literal vault like the folks at Coca-Cola, you'll keep them safe from the competition, but you'll also ensure that no one in your team ever uses them. So hopefully this goes without saying, but...
Don't do that, okay? Do not do that. Your playbooks are not family heirlooms that need to be, you know, mothballed and, you know, stored in a cool, dry location, okay? These are... living documents that need to be readily accessible and easily updated by the right people. So whatever you do, don't print them, them out and put them in a three-ring binder. I know everybody tells you to do this, but it doesn't work. All right. Formal process manuals don't work because binders and bookshelves are where documentation go to die. So again, don't do it. All right, promise me this, don't do it. Now, I probably don't have to tell you this, but we are now living in a digital first world.
And even if your team isn't fully remote, there's a good chance that some of your employees will be distributed from time to time. So for that reason alone, your playbook library needs to be digital. There are a number of great process management and company wiki tools on the market that we'll share in the resources section. But my advice is just to not overcomplicate it, okay? Starting out, you can use just a simple spreadsheet that links off to some Google Doc checklists, right? That's good enough. That's good enough for most organizations. The key is everyone must know how to quickly and easily navigate to the playbooks that matter to them. All of those boxes, then, it's probably good enough. Okay, so we've talked about the importance of training and availability in deploying your playbooks.
Now let's cover the third element. Which is accountability. I wish I could tell you that once your playbooks have been created and your team has been trained on how to use them, that nothing will ever go wrong again. That mistakes and human error, well, those are all just going to be a thing of the past. But that would be a lie, a great big fat giant lie. The fact is, things... Will still go wrong from time to time. Yep, despite all of your best documentation and training efforts, stuff will still go wrong and people will still make mistakes. So what do you do? What do you do when that happens? What do you do when something breaks? Well, anytime something goes wrong, I want you to learn to pause.
I want you to learn to pause. Ask this one simple question. Before you get upset, before you ask who's responsible, and certainly before you fire anyone, I want you to pause and I want you to ask this question. Are you ready for it? The question is... Do we have a playbook for this? That's it. That's the whole question, right? Do we have a playbook for this? Now, if the answer is no, then you know what to do next. The person who is responsible for this task needs to go back to the design step and they need to create a new playbook. Right? And this conversation doesn't have to be overly complicated, right? It can just be as simple as, 'Hey, do we have a playbook for this?' No?
Okay, well then I think we can all agree that we should. And, you know, Bob, since you're the person who's responsible for this task, I need you to create the playbook following the three... D's process. And once it's done, can we just all agree that this same mistake should never happen again? Deal, right? That's it. Then just walk your team through the 3Ds that we've already talked about, if they don't already know them, focusing on the do, document, do again, and field test steps until you have a new finish. playbook to add to your library. Now I won't lie, mistakes are a really painful way to expand your playbook library. But if you ask this question and you fo— this process, your team and your employees will be better for it because you'll be building a culture where mistakes can happen as long as one we learn from them and two we document what we learned so we don't repeat the same mistakes again and again.
And here's the harsh reality, right? I don't care how good you and your team are, no one, and I mean no one, can anticipate everything. So as your company scales, you'll find that... mistakes and screw ups become the main catalyst for playbook creation. And that's okay, right? That's okay. It just means that you're growing. So that's what you do if the answer is no, we don't have a playbook for this. But what if the answer is something more along the lines of, uh, yeah, I think maybe there's a playbook. We have one at some point. I'm not really sure. Let me check. Oh, yeah, look. Oh, wow. Magically here it is, right? Have you heard that before, right? Unfortunately, you are going to hear this answer more than you would like to hear it.
But when you do hear this answer, again, don't freak out. I want you to pause and ask this follow-up question. Are you ready? The question is: Okay. Was it followed? In other words, did the person or persons who made the mistake follow the steps that were documented in the process? If they did, and the playbook is simply out of date, then identify the playbook's owner, who again is the person who is uniquely responsible for both building and maintaining the playbook that we identified way back in the defined step, and have them work with the team to bring the playbook up to date. Bye. If the playbook is accurate and it simply wasn't followed, now we got a different issue on our hands.
I'm not going to tell you how to run your business, but barring outright negligence or just a massive lapse in judgment that completely breaks trust beyond the point of no return, we see situations like these as an opportunity for growth and learning. And no, that's not just code for fire them, right? We really do see... this as an opportunity for growth. So first, we acknowledge that a mistake was made and that there is a process in place for ensuring that mistakes like these don't happen. Then we simply remind the person that this is our process. All right. If you need additional resources and support, we're going to get them for you. We're here to help. We want to support you. At the end of the day, if you can't follow our process, you can't work here.
Now that may seem harsh, but it's actually very fair. Okay. You're not saying, you know, I don't. Like you and I think you're a bad person. Right, you're not saying, 'um,' I think you're, you know, stupid or ugly. It's none of that. Right, you're not setting them up for failure and it's not personal. All you're saying is, 'serving in this role, sitting in this seat, getting paid to do so, means that you need to execute this task according to... this process.' Now, every now and then, someone will say something like, 'but I don't want to do it that way.' I want to do it my way, to which you can respond, 'that's fine.' You can absolutely do it your way.
You just can't do it here, and you certainly can't expect to get paid for doing it your way. All right, but let's be real. The vast, vast majority of your employees, they are not going to respond like that, right? Most will be embarrassed that they messed up and they're going to feel like crap that they let you and the rest of the team down. So give some grace. Use this as an opportunity to encourage them while at the same time still speaking truth to them. Yes, a mistake was made. But mistakes happen, all right? That's what I like to tell people. Yes, a mistake was made, mistakes happen, but we still believe in you and we know that you can do great things. Just promise me that you'll follow this process moving forward. Deal? There's no subjectivity. There's no, well, the boss just doesn't like me and it's unfair. Uh-uh. All that garbage goes out the window. It's simply— this is our process, and we need you to follow it. Again, mistakes will happen. They're a fact of life. Life and they are certainly a fact of business. But mistakes are okay if one— we learn from them— and two— we create or update our processes so that the same mistake doesn't happen over and over again.
